summary: Joel Spolsky examines the failure of Clear, an airport security line-skipping service that paid for expensive background checks even after the TSA refused to let pre-screened travelers bypass security screening. Clear's original business model was to let vetted travelers skip security entirely, but when that proved impossible, they kept doing costly background checks for a service that only let people cut in line. Spolsky argues this was organizational dysfunction — the inability to adapt a business model to changed circumstances. He notes the irony that Clear might have been profitable if they had simply dropped the unnecessary background checks.
key_points: Clear's intended business model (bypassing security screening entirely) was never approved by the TSA, yet they continued operating as if it would be
The TSA's security theater is absurd — screening pilots for shampoo bombs when they already control the aircraft
Organizations can fail by refusing to re-evaluate their business model when key assumptions prove false
Clear could likely have survived as a profitable line-skipping service if they had dropped the expensive, now-purposeless background checks
Thoughtless optimism — continuing to execute a plan everyone knows is broken — is a distinct form of organizational dysfunction
money_quotes: You still went through the X-ray machine and had to remove your shoes, belt, pocket contents, laptops, and plastic quart ziplock bag of toiletries. You just got to cut to the front of the line.

The TSA doesn't even trust pilots, who go through the same screening as the rest of us to make sure they're not bringing something extraordinarily dangerous onto a plane like a 3.5 oz bottle of shampoo.

With a little bottle of shampoo, they could make a bomb, which they could use to fly the plane they are piloting into a building, something that is impossible for mere pilots sitting at the controls of the jet.
At this point, a rational businessperson would say, 'Well, does the Clear idea still make sense if we can't actually let you skip the screening?'
The environment changed. It turns out that Clear's business model of prescreening wasn't going to be possible. But they kept doing it anyway.
What kind of organizational dysfunction does it take to completely ignore the changed circumstances and keep at a money-losing business?
Clear could probably have been profitable if they had just skipped the one unnecessarily stupid part of their business model: the detailed background checks on all their customers.
They had a business model, the business model wasn't actually possible, everybody knew it, and they still plugged away at it. Thoughtless optimism.
key_insight: When a key assumption in your business model proves false, continuing to execute the original plan unchanged isn't perseverance — it's organizational dysfunction.
